map.subtitle: Big Idea 1: Creative Development   •   2 days   •   LO CRD-2.E, CRD-2.F, CRD-2.G, CRD-2.H
map.intro: Everything for Topic 1.3 lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–2 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–2 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Process Autopsy — The StudyStreak Project Log and Exercise 2: Process Doctor — Rescue the Build (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Who Really Wrote This Program? (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 2)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 9 questions across LO CRD-2.E, CRD-2.F, CRD-2.G, and CRD-2.H, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1: development processes, iterative vs. incremental, and the design pipeline from investigation to specification; Day 2: documentation, comments, and acknowledging borrowed code, closing with Common AP Traps and the exit check).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Naming: the CED topic is 'Program Design and Development'; the running example across both days is the fictional StudyStreak flashcard-app project (Day 1 phase-sort table, Day 2 acknowledgement rulings, and the exercises' six-week project log). This topic is deliberate Create Performance Task scaffolding — the process, documentation, and credit rules here are the ones the PT scores.

exercise1.title: Process Autopsy — The StudyStreak Project Log
exercise1.intro: A student team spent six weeks building StudyStreak, a flashcard app — and the demo flopped. You are the process consultant hired to do the autopsy. Their complete development log is below. For each week you will name the phase (or the missing phase), spot where iteration and testing never happened, and rule on the documentation and credit failures. Part A works the log; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives CRD-2.E, CRD-2.F, CRD-2.G, and CRD-2.H.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Development Log: Six Weeks of StudyStreak
exercise1.narrative: The team kept a short weekly log (and added their own notes in the moment). Read it week by week, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, name the phase each week's work belongs to — or the phase that is conspicuously missing — then test the team's own notes against what the CED actually says.

columns: Team's own note at the time
rows: Voted in the group chat to build a flashcard app; started coding the full app that afternoon
"We know what students want — we ARE students."
Group-chat vote, no outside input
No survey, interview, observation, or user test ever happened
Built the entire app in one push: login, decks, quiz mode, streaks, sharing
"Easier to build it all at once and test at the end."
One member's preference
No piece was verified before the next was added
Drew a paper sketch of all the screens — after the code was written — to show the teacher
"There's our design doc."
Requested by the teacher
The 'design' happened after the build it was supposed to guide
First test ever: gave the app to five students; four couldn't find the quiz button
"They're just not good with apps."
Testers recruited from study hall
Feedback was collected — and nothing was changed in response
Pasted a streak-counter code segment from a coding forum; renamed its variables
"Basically ours now."
Found by one member, merged silently
No acknowledgement appears anywhere in the program
Night before the demo, one member added comments to all 900 lines from memory
"Documentation: done."
Last-minute assignment
The only documentation of six weeks of work was written in one night, by one person

exercise1.scenarioNote: Log rows adapted from the Day 1 phase-sort table and the Day 2 acknowledgement scenarios; all terms and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which single week's decision doomed StudyStreak earliest, and which phase of a development process was skipped that week?
exercise1.partALabel: Part A - Rulings on the Log

partA.q: Week 1: name the phase the team skipped entirely, and prescribe TWO specific investigation methods from the CED they should have used — with one sentence on what each would have revealed about future users.
partA.answer: The team skipped INVESTIGATING AND REFLECTING — the first of the four commonly used phases (EK CRD-2.E.2). Per EK CRD-2.F.3, investigation can be performed by collecting data through surveys, user testing, interviews, and direct observations. Any two work: a SURVEY of students would reveal broadly what features students actually want in a study app (maybe streaks matter less than the team assumed); INTERVIEWS or DIRECT OBSERVATION of students studying would reveal how studying actually happens (constraints and user concerns, EK CRD-2.F.2). 'We ARE students' is the trap: the team investigated a sample of exactly themselves. Full credit requires the phase named plus two methods with distinct payoffs.

partA.q: Week 2: name the development approach the team rejected, state its CED definition, and explain concretely how it would have changed the Week 2 build — and why 'test it all at the end' multiplies the pain of every bug.
partA.answer: The team rejected INCREMENTAL development: a process that breaks the problem into smaller pieces and makes sure each piece works before adding it to the whole (EK CRD-2.E.4). Concretely: build login alone and verify it; then add decks and verify; then quiz mode; then streaks; then sharing — five small, individually confirmed pieces instead of one 900-line unknown. 'Test at the end' multiplies pain because when the monolith finally misbehaves, the bug could live in any piece OR in any interaction between pieces — nothing was ever confirmed working, so nothing can be ruled out. Full credit requires the term, the definition, and the isolation argument (a failure in a verified-pieces build points to the newest piece).

partA.q: Week 3: the team calls the paper sketch 'our design doc.' Using the CED's definitions of specification and the design phase, explain what's wrong with the timing — what is the design phase supposed to do, and for what?
partA.answer: Per EK CRD-2.F.6, the design phase OUTLINES HOW TO ACCOMPLISH A GIVEN PROGRAM SPECIFICATION — and the specification defines the program's requirements (EK CRD-2.F.5). Design is therefore a forward-looking blueprint: it exists to guide the build toward the specification. A sketch drawn AFTER the code is written guides nothing — it is a portrait of whatever got built, requirements or not. And since the team never investigated (Week 1), there were no real requirements and no specification for a design to accomplish in the first place. Full credit: the correct direction (design precedes and guides the build) plus the observation that the whole pipeline upstream of design was missing.

partA.q: Week 4: the team finally tested — and changed nothing. Explain what an ITERATIVE process required at this moment, quoting the CED's definition, and name the phase the team should have revisited.
partA.answer: Per EK CRD-2.E.3, an iterative process REQUIRES refinement and revision based on feedback, testing, or reflection throughout the process — and this may require revisiting earlier phases. Week 4 produced exactly the feedback iteration runs on (four of five users couldn't find the quiz button), and the required response was to revisit the DESIGNING phase and rework the interface layout. Instead the team explained the feedback away ('they're just not good with apps'), breaking the loop that makes testing worth anything. Note the subtlety: the team did perform the testing phase — what they lacked was iteration, the willingness to let test results send them backward. Full credit: definition quoted or closely paraphrased + design named as the revisit target.

exercise1.partBLabel: Part B - Reflect (AP Exam and Create PT Application)
exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: Week 5: rule on 'basically ours now.' What does the CED require for the forum code segment, exactly where must it go, and what would this exact move cost on the Create Performance Task?
partB.answer: Per EK CRD-2.H.1 it is important to acknowledge any code segment developed by another source, and renaming variables does not change the source. Per EK CRD-2.H.2 the acknowledgement can be in the program documentation and should include the ORIGIN or the ORIGINAL AUTHOR'S NAME — here, a comment above the streak counter naming the forum (or the poster) satisfies both parts. On the Create Performance Task, this exact move — uncredited use of others' work — earns a score of 0 on the entire task, not just the borrowed portion. Full credit: both halves of the rule (place + origin/author) and the PT consequence.

partB.q: Week 6: comments were added, so why does the log still show a documentation failure? Use the CED's WHEN and WHY of documentation in your ruling.
partB.answer: The WHEN failed: per EK CRD-2.G.3, programmers should document a program THROUGHOUT its development, not in one night at the end — comments reconstructed 'from memory' weeks later describe what one member guesses the code does, not what its authors knew when they wrote it. The WHY explains the cost: per EK CRD-2.G.4, documentation helps in DEVELOPING and maintaining correct programs, individually or collaboratively — documentation written after development is over already forfeited its development-time job (catching wrong assumptions, letting teammates build on each other's work). The comments aren't worthless, but they are a receipt written after the store closed. Full credit: both EK ideas applied, not just 'too late.'

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: Rewrite the six-week log as a healthy plan — one line per week — that is BOTH iterative and incremental, includes an investigation method, a specification, a testing strategy, ongoing documentation, and a properly credited borrowed segment.
partB.answer: Example plan (any equivalent earns credit): Week 1 — survey students + observe a study session; draft requirements and a one-page specification (investigating and reflecting → specification). Week 2 — storyboard screens, split the app into modules, and write the testing strategy (design phase, EK CRD-2.F.7); start the shared design log (documenting throughout). Week 3 — build and verify the deck module ONLY; document as built (incremental). Week 4 — user-test the deck module with five students; revise the storyboard based on feedback (iterative — testing sends the team back to design). Week 5 — add the quiz module, verify, integrate the forum streak-counter WITH a comment crediting the forum source (EK CRD-2.H.2). Week 6 — full-app testing pass against the Week 2 strategy; final documentation review, then demo. Strong answers show each piece verified before the next joins (incremental) and at least one explicit loop back (iterative).

exercise2.title: Process Doctor — Rescue the Build
exercise2.subtitle: 4 cases - LO CRD-2.E, CRD-2.F, CRD-2.G, CRD-2.H
exercise2.intro: You are the process doctor on call. Four troubled projects arrive. For each, name the process problem WITH CED VOCABULARY (skipped investigation, missing specification, not incremental, not iterative, documentation failure, missing acknowledgement), prescribe the fix, and justify it from the relevant EK. Advanced students also flag a second-layer issue hiding in the same story. Vague 'plan better' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): process problem named with CED vocabulary + fix + justification, for all 4 cases. Enrichment (optional): also flag the second-layer issue hiding in the same story for a more complete rescue.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every case
method: Anchor your gut diagnosis before analyzing. Writing your own read of the project's disease first keeps a convincing wrong label from swaying you.

Match the symptoms to the CED: a skipped phase (investigating and reflecting, designing, prototyping, testing), a missing specification, a build that isn't incremental, feedback that never triggers iteration, documentation that is absent or end-loaded, or borrowed code with no acknowledgement.

The fix names the phase to run or revisit and quotes the rule that justifies it: pieces are verified before joining the whole; feedback flows back into earlier phases; documentation happens throughout; credit goes in the documentation with origin or author. A fix without a justification is an incomplete answer.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (layering and limits): strong rescues state what the fix CANNOT do — an interview round fixes missing requirements but can't retroactively justify features already built; splitting a monolith into verified pieces localizes future bugs but doesn't find the current ones; crediting a borrowed segment satisfies the acknowledgement rule but does not satisfy the Create PT's separate demand that YOU can explain how the code works.
fields: Diagnosis (name the process problem with CED vocabulary):
Prescription + justification (the fix, and the EK rule that makes it the fix):

scenarios.text: A student spends a month building a locker-organizer app for teachers — layout, colors, and features all chosen because 'they seemed obvious.' At launch, zero teachers adopt it: it assumes teachers assign lockers alphabetically, but at this school lockers are assigned by grade and hallway. No teacher was ever consulted at any point.

key.signs: the fingerprints of a SKIPPED INVESTIGATING-AND-REFLECTING PHASE. The design of a program incorporates investigation to determine its requirements (EK CRD-2.F.1), and investigation exists precisely to surface program constraints and the concerns and interests of the people who will use the program (EK CRD-2.F.2). 'It seemed obvious' + a wrong core assumption (alphabetical lockers) + zero user contact = requirements that were guessed, not determined. The app isn't buggy — it's a correct build of the wrong program.
key.mechanism: run the missing phase before rebuilding: INTERVIEWS with teachers and DIRECT OBSERVATION of how lockers are actually assigned (EK CRD-2.F.3 lists surveys, user testing, interviews, direct observations), then write the requirements the investigation reveals and a specification that defines them (EK CRD-2.F.4, F.5). The justification is causal: the failure traces to a false belief about users, and investigation is the CED's named tool for replacing beliefs about users with data from users.
key.subtype: the missing-specification layer: because requirements were never determined, there was also no specification for the design phase to accomplish (EK CRD-2.F.6) — so even the month of building had no target to be measured against. 'Done' was undefined from day one. The interview round fixes future requirements but cannot retroactively justify the month already spent.

scenarios.text: A pair divides an escape-room game: one partner writes the entire 700-line game in a four-day marathon, then runs it for the first time. It crashes instantly. Every fix reveals two new failures — the timer breaks the puzzle logic, the puzzle logic breaks the scoring — and after a week of whack-a-mole they can't even say which parts have ever worked.

key.signs: a build that was never INCREMENTAL. EK CRD-2.E.4 defines incremental development as breaking the problem into smaller pieces and making sure each piece works BEFORE adding it to the whole. The giveaway symptom is the last sentence: they cannot say which parts have ever worked — in a verified-pieces build that sentence is impossible, because every piece was individually confirmed at the moment it joined.
key.mechanism: rebuild the process, not just the code: isolate the smallest piece (say, the timer), verify it alone, then add the puzzle logic and verify the pair, then scoring, and so on — each piece confirmed before the next joins (EK CRD-2.E.4). The justification is diagnostic power: when a verified-pieces build breaks, the newest piece (or its interaction with the verified whole) is the prime suspect, which converts week-long whack-a-mole into a short, ordered search.
key.subtype: the missing testing strategy: EK CRD-2.F.7 places development of a testing strategy inside the DESIGN phase — this pair's strategy ('run it once at the end') was never designed at all. Note the limit: switching to increments localizes FUTURE bugs but doesn't find the current ones; the honest prescription restarts integration from the smallest verified core rather than patching the monolith.

scenarios.text: A club's event app gets its first real users at the fall fair. Feedback is loud and consistent: the sign-up flow confuses everyone — people tap the poster image expecting it to be a button. The team logs every comment, thanks everyone warmly... and ships the spring version with the sign-up flow untouched, because 'the design phase ended in October and we're in the testing phase now.'

key.signs: a process that refuses to be ITERATIVE. EK CRD-2.E.3: an iterative process requires refinement and revision based on feedback, testing, or reflection — and this may require REVISITING EARLIER PHASES. The team collected exactly the feedback iteration runs on, then let a phase label ('design ended in October') veto the loop. Logging feedback without revising is testing theater: the testing phase ran, but its output went nowhere.
key.mechanism: reopen the design phase deliberately: take the poster-tap confusion back into UI-layout diagrams and storyboards (EK CRD-2.F.7), rework the sign-up flow, and re-test the revised prototype with fair-style users. The justification is definitional: phases are commonly used stages, not one-way gates (EK CRD-2.E.2) — the CED builds the return trip into the definition of iteration, so 'we're past design' misreads the process model itself.
key.subtype: the iteration-as-failure misconception: the team is treating a revisit as an admission that October's design was a mistake, when the CED frames revision from feedback as the process working as designed. Flag also that users tapping the poster IS a requirements discovery — the users are describing an interaction the program must provide (EK CRD-2.F.4).

scenarios.text: A solo student builds their Create PT project in a block-based environment with no comment feature, so they keep no documentation of any kind — 'not possible in this language.' The program's cleverest procedure was generated by an AI assistant; it appears nowhere in any write-up, and when asked how it works, the student shrugs: 'It works, that's what matters.' Submission is in three weeks.

key.signs: two violations wearing one excuse. First, a DOCUMENTATION failure hiding behind a missing feature: EK CRD-2.G.5 says not all programming environments support comments, so OTHER methods of documentation may be required — 'no comments possible' never becomes 'no documentation needed' (documentation is any written description of function and development, EK CRD-2.G.1). Second, an ACKNOWLEDGEMENT failure: the AI-generated procedure is a code segment developed by another source (EK CRD-2.H.1) and it is credited nowhere.
key.mechanism: two fixes, three weeks, in order. (1) Start external documentation NOW and keep it running through the remaining development (EK CRD-2.G.3): a written design log describing each procedure's function and history, exactly the 'other method' EK CRD-2.G.5 calls for. (2) Add the acknowledgement to that documentation, naming the AI tool as the origin of the procedure (EK CRD-2.H.2) — on the Create PT, uncredited use of generative AI scores 0 on the entire task, so this single missing line is the highest-stakes fix in all four cases.
key.subtype: the credited-but-not-understood layer: the College Board permits generative AI as a supplementary resource, but the student remains responsible for understanding and explaining their code — and the written responses will probe exactly that. 'It works, that's what matters' fails even after the credit line is added; the rescue plan must include the student rebuilding or line-by-line studying the AI procedure until they can explain it. Credit satisfies EK CRD-2.H.2; understanding is a separate, non-transferable debt.

discussion.title: Who Really Wrote This Program?
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 2)
discussion.overview: Every program is a pile of decisions: what to build (made before any code exists), what to write down along the way, and whose work ends up inside the final file. Across three short windows in this topic, your group will decide when planning beats experimenting, which artifact best rescues a future reader, and — the hardest one — how much of a program someone else (or an AI) can write before the word 'author' stops fitting.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Compare your bake-sale lists: which KIND of step (finding-out, planning, building, checking) showed up first and most often across the group — and which kind was missing entirely? Why do you think builders everywhere are tempted to skip that one?

Question 2. 'Just start building and figure it out as you go' versus 'plan everything first' — when has each approach actually worked in your life (a project, a game, a recipe)? Try to name the FEATURE of the situation that made that approach the right one.
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the comments misconception
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The September rescue kit
items.text: Rank these three artifacts by which does MOST to rescue September-you reading June-you's program: (1) comments explaining the tricky parts, (2) meaningful variable names throughout, (3) a separate one-page description of the program's overall plan. Defend your #1 — and name a situation where your #3 suddenly becomes the most valuable.

items.label: Task B - Documenting without comments
items.text: Your group must document a project built in a block-based environment with no comment feature. Agree on the TWO methods you would adopt and — the hard part — WHEN each happens during development, so the documentation is written throughout rather than reconstructed at the end. Record your plan.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 2, ~9 min, after the acknowledgement worked table
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The authorship line
items.text: Two Create PT submissions. Student A: 40% of the code was generated by an AI tool — every borrowed segment is credited in the documentation, and Student A can explain how every line works. Student B: 100% self-written, no borrowed code at all — but zero documentation, and Student B can no longer explain how their own cleverest procedure works. Build your group's ruling: who has the stronger claim to the word 'author' — and what does each student's file say about what authorship really is? What can credit buy, and what can it never buy?
Our group's verdict (who better earned 'author' + one-sentence justification):
